questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a energy-transfer machine to check the key physics idea. A machine gives 45 J of useful energy output from 70 J of total energy input. What is its efficiency?
questions.explanation: Efficiency equals useful energy output divided by total energy input, then multiplied by 100%.
questions.senNote: Efficiency equals useful energy output divided by total energy input, then multiplied by 100%.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a electric motor test to check the key physics idea. A machine gives 50 J of useful energy output from 80 J of total energy input. What is its efficiency?
questions.explanation: Efficiency equals useful energy output divided by total energy input, then multiplied by 100%.
questions.senNote: Efficiency equals useful energy output divided by total energy input, then multiplied by 100%.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a renewable energy debate to check the key physics idea. Which choice gives a good reason for using a renewable energy resource in the future?
options: It always gives the same power output.
It can be used without any cost at all.
It is less likely to run out.
questions.correctAnswer: It is less likely to run out.
questions.explanation: Renewable resources are replenished naturally, so they are less likely to run out.
questions.senNote: Renewable resources are replenished naturally, so they are less likely to run out.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a national grid planning meeting to check the key physics idea. Which choice gives a good reason for using a renewable energy resource in the future?
options: It always gives the same power output.
It is less likely to run out.
It produces no environmental impact anywhere.
questions.correctAnswer: It is less likely to run out.
questions.explanation: Renewable resources are replenished naturally, so they are less likely to run out.
questions.senNote: Renewable resources are replenished naturally, so they are less likely to run out.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a future energy source comparison to check the key physics idea. Which choice gives a good reason for using a renewable energy resource in the future?
options: It always gives the same power output.
It is less likely to run out.
It works only when fossil fuels are burned.
questions.correctAnswer: It is less likely to run out.
questions.explanation: Renewable resources are replenished naturally, so they are less likely to run out.
questions.senNote: Renewable resources are replenished naturally, so they are less likely to run out.

questions.correctAnswer: It is less likely to run out.
questions.explanation: Renewable resources are replenished naturally, so they are less likely to run out.
questions.senNote: Renewable resources are replenished naturally, so they are less likely to run out.

It is less likely to run out.
questions.correctAnswer: It is less likely to run out.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a school bag lift to check the key physics idea. A 2 kg bag is lifted by 3 m. Using g = 10 N/kg, what is the gain in gravitational potential energy?
questions.explanation: Gravitational potential energy equals mass x gravitational field strength x height.
questions.senNote: Gravitational potential energy equals mass x gravitational field strength x height.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a crate raised on a platform to check the key physics idea. A 3 kg bag is lifted by 4 m. Using g = 10 N/kg, what is the gain in gravitational potential energy?
questions.explanation: Gravitational potential energy equals mass x gravitational field strength x height.
questions.senNote: Gravitational potential energy equals mass x gravitational field strength x height.

questions.senNote: Gravitational potential energy equals mass x gravitational field strength x height.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a heated beaker explanation to check the key physics idea. Why does heating a substance increase its internal energy?
options: The particles become a different element.
The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a particle model description to check the key physics idea. Why does heating a substance increase its internal energy?
options: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
The substance loses all potential energy.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a thermal energy lesson to check the key physics idea. Why does heating a substance increase its internal energy?
options: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
The particles become fixed in a perfect lattice.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a temperature-change discussion to check the key physics idea. Why does heating a substance increase its internal energy?
options: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
Heating removes kinetic energy from the particles.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a heated beaker explanation to prepare for an exam-style question. Why does heating a substance increase its internal energy? Choose the best answer.
options: The atoms split into smaller nuclei.
The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a particle model description to prepare for an exam-style question. Why does heating a substance increase its internal energy? Choose the best answer.
options: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
The temperature falls whenever energy is transferred in.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a thermal energy lesson to prepare for an exam-style question. Why does heating a substance increase its internal energy?
options: The particles stop colliding with each other.
The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a temperature-change discussion to prepare for an exam-style question. Why does heating a substance increase its internal energy?
options: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a heated beaker explanation to test a common misconception. Why does heating a substance increase its internal energy?
options: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
The particles turn into electrons only.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a particle model description to test a common misconception. Why does heating a substance increase its internal energy?
options: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
The bonds disappear without any energy transfer.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a thermal energy lesson to test a common misconception. Why does heating a substance increase its internal energy? Choose the best answer.
options: The particles become a different element.
The substance loses all potential energy.
The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a temperature-change discussion to test a common misconception. Why does heating a substance increase its internal energy? Choose the best answer.
options: The particles become a different element.
The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
The particles become fixed in a perfect lattice.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a heated beaker explanation to link the context to the GCSE model. Why does heating a substance increase its internal energy? Choose the best answer.
options: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
The particles become a different element.
Heating removes kinetic energy from the particles.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles gain kinetic and potential energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of the particles in a substance.
